steps.title: Welcome to Day 13
steps.body: Customer support is one of the highest-leverage places to use AI. A single well-configured AI assistant can handle hundreds of conversations — answering FAQs, routing tickets, and drafting responses — while your human team focuses on the hard cases. Today you will learn exactly how to set that up.

steps.title: AI does not replace your support team — it multiplies them
steps.body: The goal is not to remove humans from support. It is to let AI handle the repetitive 80% — order status, return policies, password resets — so your team can spend their energy on the 20% that actually needs a person. Faster answers for customers, lighter load for your team.
highlights: your team can spend their energy on the 20%

steps.title: What good AI support looks like
steps.body: A strong AI support system does four things. First, it answers instantly — no waiting for business hours. Second, it stays on-brand — same voice, same policies, every time. Third, it knows when to escalate — handing off to a human when something is urgent, emotional, or complex. Fourth, it learns — every conversation makes it better at matching answers to questions.

steps.title: Quick check
steps.question: What is the right goal for AI in customer support?
options: Replace your entire support team with chatbots
Handle repetitive questions so humans can focus on complex cases
Send automated replies to every email without reading them
Make customers fill out forms before they can talk to a person
feedback: Exactly. AI does the repetitive work so people can do the meaningful work.
Not quite — AI handles volume, but human judgment is still essential for hard cases.
Auto-replying to everything frustrates customers. AI should route and draft, not spam.
That adds friction. Good AI support removes friction, it does not create more.

steps.title: The support AI toolkit
steps.body: You can build AI support in layers. Layer 1 — Knowledge Base: Feed your AI your FAQ, policies, and product docs so it can answer common questions accurately. Layer 2 — Email Drafting: AI reads incoming emails and drafts replies for your team to review and send. Layer 3 — Chat Routing: AI classifies incoming messages by urgency, topic, and sentiment, routing them to the right person or queue.

steps.title: Design your support prompt

fields.label: What is your business or product?
fields.placeholder: a SaaS tool for project management

fields.label: What tone should your support use?
What are your top 3 customer questions?
fields.placeholder: how to reset password, billing cycles, feature requests

fields.label: When should AI escalate to a human?
fields.placeholder: refund requests, angry tone, technical bugs

steps.template: You are a customer support specialist for {{company}}. Your tone is {{tone}}. You can answer questions about: {{common_questions}}. Important: if a customer mentions {{escalation_rule}}, do not try to solve it — escalate to a human immediately and let the customer know.

steps.title: Copy your support prompt
steps.body: You just built a reusable support prompt. Copy it and paste it into any AI tool. This is the seed of your AI support system — from here you can add more policies, more FAQs, and connect it to real tools.

steps.title: Real example: small team, big impact
steps.prompt: A 3-person SaaS company set up an AI support layer with their FAQ, return policy, and 15 common answers. Result: 70% of incoming emails got a draft reply in under 10 seconds. The team spent their time on feature requests and onboarding — not copy-pasting the same answers over and over.

steps.title: Escalate or answer?
steps.question: A customer writes: 'I have been waiting three weeks for a refund. This is unacceptable. I want to speak to a manager now.'
feedback: Correct. Angry tone + refund request = immediate human escalation. AI should acknowledge the frustration and promise a human will respond within the hour.
Think again — this is emotional, urgent, and about money. AI should not handle this.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: The best AI support systems hide the fact that AI is responding and pretend to be a real person.
feedback: Exactly — transparency builds trust. Customers should know when they are talking to AI.
Not quite. Hiding AI identity erodes trust. Customers appreciate honesty more than the illusion of a human.

steps.title: The escalation trap
steps.body: A company set up AI support but made it escalate too easily — any question with a negative word went straight to a human. The result: the AI handled almost nothing and the team was just as overloaded as before. The fix was simple: teach the AI to recognize real urgency (refund requests, security issues, legal threats) versus everyday frustration ('this is confusing, can you help?'). Precision in escalation rules makes the difference between a useful AI and an expensive redirect button.

questions.label: Think about customer support in your business or a business you know. What are the 3-5 most common questions customers ask?
Which of those questions could AI handle today? Which ones absolutely need a human? Why?

steps.title: From prompt to system
steps.body: What you built today is step one — a prompt that defines how your AI responds. The next level is connecting that prompt to real tools: your knowledge base, your ticketing system, your email. That is what we build toward in the coming days. For now, you have the foundation: a clear support voice, defined boundaries, and smart escalation rules.

steps.title: Day 13 complete
steps.body: You built your first AI support prompt and learned the core principle: AI handles the repeatable, humans handle the exceptional. You know when to escalate, how to set boundaries, and why transparency matters. Tomorrow: building an actual AI knowledge base that your support system can query in real time.
